title: Topic 184: Why Players Speedrun: The Psychology of Optimization
content:
### Abstract

This paper delves into the curious case of the modern Sisyphus: the video game speedrunner. These digital ascetics dedicate countless hours to shaving milliseconds off their completion times, a pursuit that appears, to the uninitiated, as a form of self-inflicted, pixelated torture. We explore the psychological drivers behind this phenomenon, from the intoxicating allure of the "flow state" to the surprisingly robust communities that form around these Herculean efforts. Through a synthesis of existing literature, real-world examples, and a healthy dose of academic wit, we reveal that the seemingly bizarre world of speedrunning offers profound insights into the universal human craving for optimization, mastery, and a sense of belonging. The paper argues that by understanding why someone would spend 1,000 hours to beat a game 1,000 times, we can better understand the peculiar consumer behaviors that shape our own lives, from our obsession with "life hacks" to our relentless pursuit of the "perfect" cup of coffee.

### Introduction

In the grand tapestry of human achievement, right alongside scaling Mount Everest and composing symphonies, lies a peculiar and pixelated thread: the sub-five-minute Super Mario Bros. speedrun. To the casual observer, the act of playing the same video game repeatedly, with a fervor bordering on the fanatical, might seem like a Sisyphean task for the digital age. Why would anyone subject themselves to such a grueling, repetitive endeavor? What possesses a person to spend months, even years, perfecting a run that lasts only a few minutes? This paper posits that the answer lies not in a deep-seated masochism, but in a fundamental aspect of the human psyche: the insatiable desire for optimization.

Speedrunning, the practice of completing a video game as quickly as possible, is more than just a niche hobby for the chronically over-caffeinated. It is a microcosm of our broader societal obsession with efficiency, a digital proving ground for the age-old adage, "time is money," even when the only currency is bragging rights and a place on a leaderboard. This paper will explore the psychological underpinnings of this fascinating subculture, examining the intricate interplay of intrinsic and extrinsic motivations that drive players to push the boundaries of what is possible within a game's code. We will delve into the concept of the "flow state," that elusive mental space where action and awareness merge, and explore how the vibrant communities that spring up around these games provide a sense of purpose and belonging that transcends the solitary nature of the task itself. Ultimately, by dissecting the motivations of the speedrunner, we can gain a deeper understanding of our own consumer behaviors, our relentless pursuit of the "best" and the "fastest," and the myriad ways in which we seek to optimize our own lives, one saved second at a time.

### Literature Review

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not entirely ignored the siren song of the video game. A veritable treasure trove of research exists on the psychological impacts of gaming, though the specific sub-basement of speedrunning has remained relatively unexplored, a dusty corner of the library where only the most dedicated (or perhaps misguided) scholars dare to tread. The existing literature, however, provides a fertile ground for our investigation, offering tantalizing glimpses into the minds of those who choose to live their lives one frame at a time.

The concept of "flow," as articulated by the eminent psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, is perhaps the most well-trodden path in the study of gaming psychology. This state of "optimal experience," where one is fully immersed in an activity, feeling energized, focused, and fully involved, is the holy grail for many a gamer. For the speedrunner, achieving a state of flow is not just a pleasant side effect; it is a prerequisite for success. As one article eloquently puts it, top runners reach a "flow state that can only come from hours and hours of perfecting game mechanics" [1]. This is the runner's high of the digital age, a state of pure, unadulterated focus where the outside world melts away, leaving only the player, the controller, and the relentless march of the in-game timer.

Of course, the path to flow is not without its perils. The literature is rife with studies on the darker side of gaming, with researchers diligently cataloging the correlation between excessive video game use and a veritable smorgasbord of psychological maladies, including depression, anxiety, and social phobia [2]. However, as any seasoned speedrunner will tell you, correlation does not equal causation. It is just as likely that individuals prone to these conditions are drawn to the structured, predictable world of video games as a form of escapism. The speedrunning community, with its vibrant online forums, live-streaming platforms, and collaborative spirit, can actually serve as a powerful antidote to social isolation, a digital agora where like-minded individuals can connect and commiserate over a missed glitch or a personal best.

Furthermore, the very nature of speedrunning, with its emphasis on self-improvement and mastery, aligns perfectly with the principles of Self-Determination Theory. This theory posits that humans have three innate psychological needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Speedrunning, in its own peculiar way, manages to satisfy all three. The player has complete autonomy over their chosen game and strategy, the pursuit of a world record provides a clear path to demonstrating competence, and the community provides a sense of relatedness and shared purpose. It is a self-contained ecosystem of motivation, a perfect feedback loop of challenge and reward that keeps players coming back for more, long after the initial novelty of the game has worn off.

### Analysis

To truly understand the speedrunner, we must dissect the very engine that drives them: the paradoxical psychology of optimization. This is a realm where the concepts of “fun” and “work” become hopelessly entangled, where the pursuit of an arbitrary goal is elevated to an art form. It is here, in the crucible of countless failed attempts and fleeting moments of triumph, that we can observe the fundamental principles of human motivation in their purest, most pixelated form.

The speedrunner is a creature of two worlds, straddling the line between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation with the grace of a seasoned platforming hero. The intrinsic drive, the pure, unadulterated joy of mastery, is the bedrock of their obsession. It is the satisfaction of executing a difficult trick perfectly, of seeing one's own skills improve over time, of bending the very fabric of the game to one's will. This is the “task-oriented” motivation that researchers speak of, the desire to improve for the sake of improvement itself [3]. It is the quiet satisfaction of a personal best, a victory that needs no audience, no fanfare, only the quiet hum of a well-loved console.

But let us not be naive. The speedrunner is not a monk, meditating in a digital monastery. They are also driven by the siren song of extrinsic motivation, the allure of the leaderboard, the roar of the Twitch chat, the coveted title of “world record holder.” This is the “ego-oriented” motivation, the desire to be the best, to see one's name in lights, to be recognized by one's peers as a master of the craft. The two are not mutually exclusive; in fact, they are often intertwined, a double-helix of motivation that propels the speedrunner to ever-greater heights. The desire to be the best (ego) fuels the long hours of practice required to achieve mastery (task), and the satisfaction of mastery (task) makes the pursuit of the top spot (ego) all the more rewarding.

The elusive “flow state,” as previously mentioned, is the psychological lubricant that makes this whole endeavor possible. It is a state of such intense focus that the speedrunner becomes one with the game, their fingers moving with a preternatural grace, their decisions made in a fraction of a second. It is a state of “optimal experience,” a perfect balance between challenge and skill that is so engrossing, so utterly captivating, that the speedrunner can lose all track of time, emerging from their digital trance hours later, exhausted but exhilarated. This is the addictive quality of speedrunning, the psychological carrot that keeps them coming back for more, chasing the dragon of the perfect run.

And what of the cognitive biases that plague us mere mortals? The speedrunner is not immune. The “sunk cost fallacy,” in particular, looms large in the world of speedrunning. After investing hundreds, even thousands, of hours into a single game, the thought of abandoning the pursuit can be unbearable. “I’ve come this far,” the speedrunner tells themselves, “I can’t give up now.” And so they press on, driven not just by the promise of future glory, but by the weight of past investment. It is a powerful, if not entirely rational, motivator, a testament to the human tendency to throw good money (or in this case, time) after bad.

### Real-World Examples

Lest this paper devolve into a purely theoretical treatise, a dry and dusty tome of academic jargon, let us now turn our attention to the real world, where the psychology of speedrunning plays out in all its glorious, glitch-filled absurdity. For it is here, in the annals of gaming history, that we find the most compelling evidence for our thesis, the most vivid illustrations of the human capacity for dedication, innovation, and the occasional, game-breaking exploit.

Consider, for a moment, the “four-minute mile” of the speedrunning world: the sub-five-minute *Super Mario Bros.* run. For years, this was considered the holy grail, a seemingly insurmountable barrier that separated the merely great from the truly legendary. The quest to break this barrier was a marathon of its own, a multi-year effort that saw the record inch ever closer to the magical five-minute mark. When it was finally broken, the speedrunning community erupted in a collective roar of triumph. It was a testament to the power of perseverance, a demonstration of what can be achieved when a community bands together to push the boundaries of the possible. It was also, let’s be honest, a little bit insane.

And then there is Games Done Quick (GDQ), the biannual charity marathon that has become the speedrunning world’s answer to the Olympics. Here, in a hotel ballroom filled with the glow of a thousand screens, the best and brightest of the speedrunning community gather to showcase their skills, raise money for charity, and generally revel in their shared obsession. GDQ is a beautiful, chaotic spectacle, a place where you can witness a blindfolded run of *Mike Tyson’s Punch-Out!!* followed by a four-way race through the entirety of the *Final Fantasy* series. It is a powerful demonstration of the community aspect of speedrunning, a reminder that even the most solitary of pursuits can foster a sense of belonging and camaraderie. It is also, and this cannot be overstated, a place where you can watch a grown man in a dinosaur onesie scream at a television for seven days straight, all in the name of a good cause.

No discussion of real-world examples would be complete without a nod to the glitches, the happy accidents of code that have become the speedrunner’s best friend. The “backwards long jump” in *Super Mario 64*, a physics-defying exploit that allows Mario to accelerate to ludicrous speeds, is a prime example. The discovery of this glitch revolutionized the *Mario 64* speedrunning scene, shaving minutes off the world record and opening up a whole new world of possibilities. It is a testament to the creativity and ingenuity of the speedrunning community, a reminder that sometimes, the best way to move forward is to go backwards, very, very fast.

### Discussion

The world of speedrunning, with its arcane rituals and seemingly nonsensical objectives, may appear to be a hermetically sealed bubble, a strange and wonderful subculture with little relevance to the outside world. But to dismiss it as such would be to miss the point entirely. For in the relentless pursuit of the perfect run, we can see a reflection of our own, often equally absurd, quest for optimization in the broader consumer landscape. The speedrunner, in their own peculiar way, is the ultimate consumer, the ultimate “life hacker,” constantly searching for the most efficient path, the most optimal strategy, the perfect, glitch-assisted shortcut to success.

Consider the modern obsession with “life hacking,” the endless stream of books, blogs, and podcasts dedicated to helping us optimize every conceivable aspect of our lives. From the “Pomodoro Technique” for maximizing productivity to the “KonMari Method” for decluttering our homes, we are constantly bombarded with messages that tell us we can, and should, be doing things better, faster, and more efficiently. The speedrunner is simply taking this logic to its absurd, yet logical, conclusion. They are not just optimizing their work or their home; they are optimizing their play, turning a leisure activity into a high-stakes race against the clock. In this, they are a funhouse mirror reflection of our own consumerist tendencies, our desire to quantify, measure, and ultimately conquer every aspect of our lives.

The “gamification” of life is another area where the parallels are striking. From fitness apps that award badges for reaching a certain number of steps to language-learning programs that use points and leaderboards to motivate users, we are increasingly turning to game-like mechanics to make the mundane more engaging. The speedrunner is simply ahead of the curve. They have been gamifying their own lives for years, turning the solitary act of playing a video game into a global competition, complete with its own set of rules, its own community, and its own, highly coveted, set of rewards. They are the pioneers of a world where everything is a game, and every game is a competition.

Of course, this relentless focus on optimization is not without its downsides. The speedrunner, in their quest for the perfect run, can easily fall prey to burnout, frustration, and the gnawing sense that their efforts are, in the grand scheme of things, utterly meaningless. The same can be said for our own, more socially acceptable, forms of optimization. The pursuit of the perfect career, the perfect body, the perfect life, can be an equally thankless task, a never-ending treadmill of self-improvement that leaves us feeling more exhausted than exhilarated. The speedrunner, then, serves as a cautionary tale, a reminder that the pursuit of perfection is a marathon, not a sprint, and that sometimes, the most optimal strategy is to simply slow down and enjoy the game.

### Conclusion

In the final analysis, what can we conclude about the strange and wonderful world of speedrunning? What have we learned from these digital ascetics, these masters of the pixelated domain? We have learned that the human desire for optimization knows no bounds, that we will, if given the chance, turn even our leisure activities into a high-stakes race against the clock. We have learned that the pursuit of mastery, the intoxicating allure of the “flow state,” can be a powerful motivator, capable of driving individuals to feats of dedication that border on the superhuman. And we have learned that even the most solitary of pursuits can foster a sense of community, a sense of belonging, a sense of shared purpose that transcends the digital divide.

The speedrunner, then, is not an anomaly, not a glitch in the human matrix. They are, in fact, the most human of us all. They are the embodiment of our relentless drive to improve, to innovate, to push the boundaries of what is possible. They are the modern-day Sisyphus, but with a crucial difference: they are not condemned to their task; they have chosen it. And in that choice, in that absurd, beautiful, and utterly pointless dedication to the perfect run, we can see a reflection of ourselves, our own hopes, our own dreams, our own, uniquely human, obsession with the game of life.
references:
[1] Toftness, A. (2020, February 7). The Psychology of Speedrunning. Alexander Toftness. https://www.alexandertoftness.com/blog/2020/2/4/the-psychology-of-speedrunning

[2] Howard, D. R. (2021, May 15). Do Speedrunners Experience Flow?. Medium. https://rhombical.medium.com/do-speedrunners-experience-flow-69da6d52be60

[3] Lenti, E. (2021, July 10). Why Do Gamers Love Speedrunning So Much Anyway?. Wired. https://www.wired.com/story/why-gamers-love-speedrunning/
